planGoal: Reduce pain and sensitivity in the proximal hamstring by introducing isometric and slow isotonic loading. Maintain cardiovascular fitness through low-impact activity. Establish a consistent daily routine.

runningAllowance.guidance: Short, flat run-walk intervals only. Stop if pain exceeds 4/10 during or rises above 5/10 the morning after.
protocol: Run 2 minutes, walk 2 minutes — repeat 4 times
Flat surface only, easy conversational pace
If pain-free after session: you may add 1 additional interval next session
Stop immediately if you feel sharp or worsening pain

warmUp: 5-minute brisk walk to raise tissue temperature
Standing hip circles — 10 each direction, both sides
Bodyweight mini squat — 10 slow reps, focus on control

exercises.name: Isometric hamstring hold (supine)
exercises.tempo: Press steadily into the floor, hold for 45 seconds, release slowly
exercises.painRule: Keep pain at or below 4/10 during the hold. If pain exceeds 4/10, reduce effort by 20%. Stop if pain is sharp.
exercises.reason: Isometric loading reduces pain sensitivity in tendons without provoking them. This is the safest starting point for tendinopathy.
instructions: Lie on your back with knees bent to about 90°, feet flat on the floor
Press your heels firmly into the floor as if trying to drag them toward you — your hips should stay down
Hold this pressing effort for 45 seconds, breathing steadily throughout
Release slowly and rest for 60 seconds before the next hold
You should feel the effort in the back of your thigh, not in your lower back

exercises.name: Glute bridge
exercises.tempo: 2 seconds up, 1 second hold, 2 seconds down
exercises.painRule: Mild discomfort (1–3/10) is acceptable. Stop if pain spikes above 4/10.
exercises.reason: Activates the gluteals and offloads some demand from the hamstring tendon.
instructions: Lie on your back with knees bent, feet hip-width apart and flat on the floor
Squeeze your glutes and push your hips upward over 2 seconds until your body forms a straight line from shoulders to knees
Hold at the top for 1 second, keeping your core lightly engaged
Lower your hips slowly back to the floor over 2 seconds
Avoid arching your lower back at the top — focus on squeezing the glutes

exercises.name: Calf raises (bilateral)
exercises.painRule: Should be pain-free. This is a maintenance exercise for the lower limb.
exercises.reason: Maintains calf and Achilles capacity while overall load is reduced.
instructions: Stand with feet hip-width apart, toes pointing forward
Hold a wall or chair lightly for balance if needed
Rise up onto the balls of both feet over 2 seconds, going as high as comfortable
Lower slowly back to the floor over 2 seconds — don't drop
Keep your weight evenly spread across both feet throughout

warmUp: Standing hip swings — 10 forward/back each leg
Glute bridge hold — 1 × 20-second hold at low effort
Calf raises — 10 slow reps to activate the lower limb

exercises.name: Hamstring curl (prone, bodyweight or light resistance band)
exercises.tempo: 3 seconds lowering, 1 second hold, 2 seconds up
exercises.painRule: Pain must stay at or below 4/10 throughout. If it exceeds this, reduce range of motion or remove the band.
exercises.reason: Slow tempo puts the tendon under time under tension, which is the key stimulus for tendon remodelling.
instructions: Lie face down on the floor with legs straight and arms resting under your forehead
If using a resistance band, loop it around one ankle with the other end anchored to something in front of you
Bend your knee, bringing your heel toward your glutes over 2 seconds
Hold for 1 second at the top, then lower slowly over 3 seconds back to the start
Keep your hips flat on the floor — avoid letting them twist or lift

exercises.name: Single-leg deadlift (bodyweight)
exercises.tempo: 3 seconds to lower, 2 seconds to return
exercises.painRule: Mild ache at sit bone (1–3/10) acceptable. Stop if pain is sharp or exceeds 4/10. Avoid if hip compression is painful.
exercises.reason: Loads the hamstring in a functional position. Use a wall for balance if needed.
instructions: Stand on one leg with a slight bend in the knee, hands at your sides
Hinge forward at the hip over 3 seconds, letting your free leg extend behind you for counterbalance
Lower your torso until it's roughly parallel to the floor, or as far as you can go without pain
Drive through the heel of your standing leg to return upright over 2 seconds
Use a wall or chair for light balance support if needed — this is fine and encouraged early on

exercises.name: Side-lying hip abduction
exercises.reason: Strengthens the hip abductors, which reduce compressive load on the proximal hamstring during running.
instructions: Lie on your side with your body in a straight line, bottom arm extended under your head
Stack your hips — make sure you're not leaning forward or backward
Keeping your top leg straight and foot flexed (toes pointing forward), lift it toward the ceiling over 2 seconds
Hold briefly at the top, then lower slowly over 2 seconds
Avoid rolling your hips backward to gain height — keep the movement controlled

warmUp: 10-minute brisk walk — slightly longer today ahead of run preparation
Standing hip circles — 10 each direction, both sides
Step touches side to side — 20 reps, slow and controlled

exercises.name: Hamstring curl (prone)
exercises.tempo: 3 seconds lowering, 1 second hold, 2 seconds up
exercises.painRule: Pain at or below 4/10. Same rules as Day 3.
exercises.reason: Building on Day 3 loading. Additional set increases stimulus gently.
instructions: Lie face down on the floor with legs straight and arms resting under your forehead
Bend your knee, bringing your heel toward your glutes over 2 seconds
Hold for 1 second at the top, then lower slowly over 3 seconds back to the start
Keep your hips flat on the floor — avoid letting them twist or lift
This session adds a 4th set — rest 90 seconds between sets

exercises.name: Glute bridge with 3-second hold
exercises.tempo: 2 seconds up, 3 seconds hold, 2 seconds down
exercises.painRule: Mild discomfort acceptable. Sharp pain = stop.
exercises.reason: Progressing the isometric demand of the bridge to build tolerance.
instructions: Lie on your back with knees bent, feet hip-width apart and flat on the floor
Squeeze your glutes and push your hips upward over 2 seconds
Hold at the top for a full 3 seconds — keep squeezing your glutes throughout
Lower your hips slowly back to the floor over 2 seconds
The longer hold increases the isometric challenge compared to Day 1

exercises.name: Step-up (low step, ~15cm)
exercises.tempo: Controlled and deliberate, 2 seconds per step
exercises.painRule: Pain should stay below 4/10. If pain at sit bone worsens, reduce step height.
exercises.reason: Functional loading pattern that mimics the demands of running.
instructions: Stand facing a low step or box (around 15 cm / 6 inches high)
Place one foot fully on the step, with your whole foot on the surface
Press through the heel of the raised foot to drive your body up over 2 seconds
Step back down with control — lead with the same foot you stepped up with
Complete all 10 reps on one leg before switching sides

mobilityRecovery: Gentle hip flexor stretch (not hamstring stretching) — hold 30 seconds, 2 sets
Foam roll the glutes and TFL — avoid rolling directly on the hamstring origin
Ice the sit bone area for 10 minutes post-session if tender
Light walking (20–30 minutes) on rest days to maintain circulation
educationNotes: Proximal hamstring tendinopathy responds well to load — the key is applying the right amount at the right pace.
Pain during or after exercise is expected and acceptable up to 4/10. Pain above this, or next-morning stiffness above 5/10, means you did too much.
Avoid aggressive stretching of the hamstring — it compresses the tendon origin and can worsen symptoms.
Sitting for long periods can provoke symptoms — use a cushion or raise hips above knees when seated.
progressionRules: Progress to the next phase if: pain during exercise stays below 3/10 for 2 consecutive sessions, next-morning stiffness is below 3/10, and you can complete all sets with good form.
Increase running volume by no more than 10% per week.
Only add resistance to exercises once bodyweight versions are pain-free.
stopOrEscalateRules: Stop any exercise if pain exceeds 4/10 during the movement.
If next-morning stiffness is above 5/10 after a session, reduce load by 30% at the next session.
See a physiotherapist if: pain is not improving after 3 weeks, neurological symptoms develop (numbness, tingling), or pain exceeds 7/10 at rest.
warnings: This plan is educational guidance only. It does not replace assessment by a physiotherapist.
If your symptoms change, worsen, or new symptoms develop, stop and seek clinical advice.
